explanation_md: `m_count` starts at 3 (the in-class initializer). Each call to `increment()` adds 1, so after two calls it is 5. The `getCount()` accessor returns that value and `std::cout` prints `5` followed by a newline.

prompt_md: Which statement best describes the default access level for members declared inside a `class` (not a `struct`) in C++?

options.d: There is no default; you must always specify an access specifier.

explanation_md: In a `class`, members are `private` by default â€” the opposite of `struct`, where members are `public` by default. This is, in fact, the only language-level difference between `class` and `struct` in C++.

options.b: `m_value` is private (class default) so a free function cannot access it.

explanation_md: Because `class` defaults to private, `m_value` is private. `printValue` is a free function â€” it is not a member of `Box` â€” so it cannot access `m_value` directly. The fix is to call `b.getValue()` instead, which is the public accessor.

prompt_md: Inside a `Score` member function, can you read the `private` member of a *different* `Score` object passed as a parameter?

options.a: No â€” private means per-object, so only `this` object's private members are accessible.
options.b: Yes â€” access is per-class, so any `Score` member function can read private members of any `Score` object.
options.c: Only if the parameter is passed by value, not by reference.
options.d: Only if you cast the parameter to `void*` first.

explanation_md: Access control in C++ is per-class, not per-object. A member function of `Score` can access the private members of any `Score` object it can see â€” including a separate instance passed as a parameter. This is how comparison member functions (like `isHigherThan`) are typically written.

`Point` is a `struct`, so `x` and `y` are public and can be read directly. `Pixel` is a `class` with private members; the `print()` member function accesses them legally from inside the class. Both output lines print the stored values separated by a space.
